HP's design language doesn't change often, and this is another silver laptop made from aluminum, so it looks and feels premium, but it's arguably too subdued. Because of the premium build quality and powerful specs, it's a somewhat heavy machine at 5.17 pounds. On the other hand, the ports are very good. With two Thunderbolt 4 and two USB Type-A ports, HDMI, a headphone jack, and a microSD card reader, you have plenty of options for connecting peripherals to your laptop. You can bypass docking stations with all the built-in ports, but having Thunderbolt does give you expansion options if you need them.

For what it offers, the HP Envy 16 isn't overly expensive, and it's an easy recommendation.

HP Pavilion Aero 13 (2023)
Best affordable lightweight laptop

Lightweight and capable at a reasonable price

$580 $880 Save $300

If you want a lightweight laptop that isn't overly expensive, the HP Pavilion Aero 13 is a fantastic choice. With AMD Ryzen 7000 series processors and a 16:10 display, you're getting great performance and multitasking capabilities.

Pros
  • Powerful AMD Ryzen 7035 series processors
  • Tall 16:10 display with optional Quad HD+ resolution
  • It weighs under 2.2 pounds and comes in four colors
Cons
  • 720p webcam
  • Newer models have been rising in price
$580 at HP $930 at HP (customizable) $800 at Amazon

If the laptops above are a little too expensive for you, the HP Pavilion Aero 13 is a fantastic option that's more affordable while still being very capable and surprisingly lightweight.

The latest model comes with powerful AMD Ryzen 7035 processors, up to a Ryzen 7 7735U with 8 cores and 16 threads. It offers really strong performance, and it's a major upgrade from previous models, especially in terms of integrated graphics, which are good enough for light gaming now. Combined with up to 16GB of RAM and a 1TB SSD, it makes for a great experience.

HP hasn't made many changes to the display, so we're looking at a 13.3-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio. The base model comes in Full HD+ resolution, but you can upgrade to a Quad HD+ panel, which makes this an excellent choice at this price point. The downside here is the webcam, which is still a 720p sensor without facial recognition, a clear step down from HP's more premium laptops. There's a fingerprint reader for Windows Hello, at least.

Another great thing about this laptop is the design. For starters, it weighs just under 2.2 pounds, which is super light for such a cheap laptop, and that's on top of coming in four colors to choose from. It has a fairly basic design, but these color options make help it stand out. And for ports, you get one USB Type-C and two Type-A ports, HDMI, and a headphone jack, which is a solid setup.

HP has been increasing the price of the Pavilion Aero over the past couple of years, but this is still a great and relatively affordable laptop. Sales have been frequent with past models, so it's worth keeping an eye out if you're interested in saving more money. We reviewed the original model back in 2021 if you'd like to know more.

HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook
Best Chromebook

Powerful processors and a stunning display

HP makes some of the best Chromebooks around, and the Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is no exception. With top-tier Intel processors, a bright display, and even some RGB lighting, this is an exceptional machine if you want a Chromebook. Unfortunately, it's pricier than most Chrome OS laptops.

Pros
  • 12th-generation Intel CPUs delivering fantastic performance on Chrome OS
  • Sharp Quad HD+ display with a whopping 1,200 nits of brightness
  • 8MP webcam
Cons
  • A little heavier than it could be
  • Very expensive for a Chromebook
$1000 at HP

HP has been going strong with its Chromebooks, and the latest Dragonfly Pro Chromebook, despite the odd naming convention, is a great example of why. It's a fantastic machine in almost every way, with only a couple of notable downsides.

For starters, performance is handled by a 12th-generation Intel Core i5-1235U, a processor with 10 cores and 12 threads that can boost up to 4.4GHz. It's not the cream of the crop anymore, but it's far from a slow processor, and more importantly, it's perfect for ChromeOS, which is a much lighter operating system than Windows. The only configuration available also includes 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD, rounding out a great experience.

One of the highlights of this laptop is the display, which is a 14-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio and super-sharp Quad HD+ resolution. That's already great, but what's even more special is that this display can reach an impressive 1,200 nits of brightness, making it easily visible just about anywhere. Likewise, the webcam is one of the best you'll see on any laptop thanks to the 8MP sensor. ChromeOS doesn't support facial recognition for login, but the laptop has a fingerprint reader instead.

As far as the design goes, the HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook looks great in both Ceramic White and Sparkling Black, and it even has a cool RGB-backlit keyboard to give it a bit of extra flair. One downside is that it's a little heavy considering the size, coming in at 3.33 pounds. It's not super heavy, but not as portable as it could be. It also doesn't have a ton of ports, though it does include a whopping four Thunderbolt 4 connections. However, it lacks things like a headphone jack, so you may need some adapters.

Despite some design blunders, the HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is a fantastic laptop. Aside from being a little heavy, the biggest downside is that it's expensive, coming in at $1,000. However, it's easily the best ChromeOS experience the company offers.

HP Chromebook Plus 15a
Best affordable Chromebook

Solid performance and premium design at a great price

$349 $499 Save $150

Despite the affordable price tag, the HP Chromebook Plus 15a is a very capable ChromeOS laptop, featuring an 8-core processor, along with 8GB of RAM and 128GB of UFS storage. It also has a large Full HD display and a premium design using recycled aluminum.

Pros
  • 8-core Intel processor delivers solid performance for everyday use
  • Large Full HD display isn't always a guarantee at this price
  • Sleek design with recycled aluminum for a premium feel
Cons
  • Not the lightest laptop around
$349 at Best Buy $430 at HP

HP makes a ton of Chromebooks, and if the Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is too expensive for you, the recently-launched HP Chromebook Plus 15a is an excellent alternative.

Inside this laptop is an Intel Core i3-N305 processor, which is a low-power CPU with eight cores and eight threads. That's more than enough for solid performance in day-to-day tasks, and it comes, especially with a lightweight platform like ChromeOS. Plus, it has 8GB of RAM and 128GB of UFS storage, so it has great performance for the price.

The display is solid, too. At this price range, it's not always guaranteed you'll get a Full HD panel, especially on a 15-inch laptop, but that's exactly what you get here, which is great to see. What's even rarer is a 1080p webcam, but that's included, too, so it's really hard to complain about what you're getting at this price.

Coming around to the design, the laptop features a sleek silver look with a chassis that uses recycled aluminum and plastic for a laptop that still feels premium despite its low price. It weighs 3.81 pounds, which is reasonable for a 15-inch laptop. As for ports, it has two USB-C and one USB Type-A, plus a headphone jack and a microSD card reader.

For $500, this is a very good laptop, but if you can find it on sale (as it is at writing time), then it's an absolute steal.

Best HP laptops in 2023: Bottom line

These are the best laptops you can get from HP right now. Whether it's a gift for a loved one or for yourself, these are all fantastic options for different types of users. For most people, the HP Spectre x360 13.5 is likely the best because it's got it all: Great performance, a stunning display that's even better with the OLED model, and a great webcam, all packed in a beautiful premium design that's not overly heavy.

HP Spectre x360 13.5 (2023)
Best overall
$900 $1500 Save $600

The 2023 HP Spectre x360 13.5 packs 13th-generation Intel Core processors in a sleek and stylish design. It also has a great 13.5-inch display with a 3:2 aspect ratio that makes it optimal for productivity.

$900 at Best Buy

But of course, there's something for everyone, and if your budget is a little tighter, it's always worth looking at the Pavilion Aero, which offers absolutely stellar value for its price. It is a bit more expensive than its predecessors, but this year's model is also significantly more powerful thanks to AMD's Ryzen 7035 series processor, while still having a great display and a very lightweight design. If you want to save even more and you don't need anything fancy, you can go with the HP Chromebook x360 13.3, too.

We included a couple of cellular-connected laptops on this list, but if you're looking for more, check out our list of the best 5G laptops you can buy today. There are plenty of great options there too.
item.content:encoded:
Few brands in the laptop world are as renowned and popular as HP. From budget-friendly laptops to high-end premium experiences, the company offers some of the best laptops you can buy. If you're looking to upgrade your current personal or work computer, or you want to gift someone else, going with HP is definitely a smart move. But there are plenty of options to choose from in the company's lineup and choosing the right one for you may prove difficult. To help you with that, we've rounded up the best HP laptops you can buy today.

HP covers a lot of ground, and whether you want a Windows 11 laptop or a Chromebook, there's something for you. We've chosen models geared at all kinds of different users, so whatever you're looking for, we've got you covered.

  • HP Spectre x360 13.5 (2023)
    Best overall
    $900 at Best Buy
  • Omen 17 (Intel, 2023)
    Best gaming laptop
    $1556 at Amazon
  • HP Dragonfly G4
    Best business laptop
    $1401 at HP
  • HP Spectre x360 16 (2023)
    Best 15/16-inch laptop
    $1200 at HP
  • HP Envy 17 (2023)
    Best 17-inch laptop
    $650 at HP
  • HP Envy 16 (2023)
    Best laptop for creators
    $1199 at B&H
  • HP Pavilion Aero 13 (2023)
    Best affordable lightweight laptop
    $580 at HP
  • HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook
    Best Chromebook
    $1000 at HP
  • HP Chromebook Plus 15a
    Best affordable Chromebook
    $349 at Best Buy

Our top picks for HP laptops in 2023

HP Spectre x360 13.5 (2023)
Best overall

A premium laptop with very few flaws

$900 $1500 Save $600

The 2023 HP Spectre x360 13.5 packs 13th-generation Intel Core processors in a sleek and stylish design. It also has a great 13.5-inch display with a 3:2 aspect ratio that makes it optimal for productivity.

Pros
  • Great performance and battery life with 13th-gen Intel processors
  • Premium all-aluminum design with a dual-tone look
  • Tall 3:2 display is great for working on the go
  • 5MP webcam
Cons
  • 5G support not yet available for this year's model
$950 at HP $900 at Best Buy $1350 at Best Buy (OLED)

The HP Spectre x360 is one of the most iconic Windows laptops out there, and with the new Spectre x360 13.5, it's just as easy to recommend as previous models.

Performanmce-wise, the HP Spectre x360 13.5 is packing up to a 13th-generation Intel Core i7-1355U, with 10 cores and 12 threads delivering strong performance and great battery life for a Windows laptop. You also get 16GB of RAM and up to a 1TB SSD for storage, though more configurations are likely to show up over time.

The 13.5-inch display on the HP Spectre x360 is also great, featuring a 3:2 aspect ratio and Full HD+ resolution in the base model. You have the option to get a 3K2K OLED panel, too, which is even sharper and produces extremely vibrant colors and pure blacks, making for a fantastic viewing experience on top of being a great canvas for work. Above the display is a 5MP webcam, which continues to be one of the best you can find on any laptop, and it also supports Windows Hello. It has some smart features, too, like auto framing and lighting correction.

HP hasn't really changed the design for this year, so we get the typical dual-tone look in both Nightfall Black and Nocturne Blue colorways.It looks great, while still being somewhat subdued. Being an all-aluminum laptop, the SPectre x360 13.5 weighs in at 3.01 pounds. A single-tone Natural Silver model should also be available at some point. As for ports, we're looking at two Thunderbolt 4 connections, USB Type-A, a headphone jack, and a microSD card reader, a solid setup for a laptop of this size.

All in all, this is a fantastic premium convertible that gives you a tall high-quality screen, a striking premium design, and great performance all around. It's definitely one of the best laptops HP makes.

Omen 17 (Intel, 2023)
Best gaming laptop

Powerful performance and a smooth display

$1556 $1700 Save $144

The Omen 17 is HP's most powerful gaming laptop, with 13th-generation Intel HX-series processors and up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4090 Laptop GPU delivering outstanding performance. You also get a great display for gaming and up to 32GB of RAM.

Pros
  • Top-tier performance with 13th-gen Intel CPUs and Nvidia RTX 40 series GPUs
  • Sleek design with RGB for some flair
  • Plenty of ports
Cons
  • Battery life is abysmal
  • Still using a 720p webcam without Windows Hello
$1700 at HP (Core i7) $2600 at HP (Core i9) $1556 at Amazon

HP also caters to gamers with its Omen lineup of laptops, and the Omen 17 is the cream of the crop if you want to get into gaming.

Performance is the name of the game here, and with up to an Intel Core i9-13900HX with 24 cores and 32 threads, the Omen 17 is off to a very good start. What's more, that's paired with up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4090 Laptop GPU, giving you enough power to run just about any modern game with no issues. You also get up 32GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD.

The display is a 17.3-inch panel and it comes in the typical 16:9 aspect ratio, which is becoming less common. TYThe base model is Full HD and has a 144Hz refresh rate, but you can upgrade to a Quad HD panel and choose either 165Hz or 240Hz models depending on how smooth you want your games to be. Both options are very good, though. The same can't be said for the webcam, which is still a 720p camera with no Windows Hello support.

In terms of design, the Omen 17 looks fairly sleek and clean, and even though it does have RGB, it's done in a very subdued way so it's not obnoxious at all. It's still a heavy laptop, though, starting at 6.1 pounds, and that's to be expected with such powerful specs. You also get a healthy supply of ports. There are three USB Type-A ports (which many peripherals still use), a Thunderbolt 4/USB-C port, HDMI 2.1, mini DisplayPort, Gigabit Ethernet, microSD card reader, and a headphone jack. Plus, of course, a proprietary power connector.

Overall, the Omen 17 is a beastly gaming laptop with a sleek, albeit heavy design. But if you want the most power in a form factor that you can take with you more easily, this is the best laptop HP makes.

HP Dragonfly G4
Best business laptop

One of the sleekest business laptops around

$1401 $2548 Save $1147

The HP Dragonfly G4 looks just like its predecessor but that's not a bad thing. It has a sleek design and a tall 3:2 display including an optional privacy screen, and it has a 5MP webcam. This model comes with upgraded processors that deliver even better performance than last year's.

$1401 at HP $1655 at B&H $1409 at Walmart

Business laptops are one of HP's strong suits, and the Dragonfly G4 is the latest entry in the company's most premium family, which was called Elite Dragonfly until last year. Not much has changed for this year's model, but it didn't need to, considering its predecessor was already great.

The major change for this year is on the inside, with new 13th-generation Intel Core processors, up to a Core i7-1365U with vPro. It comes with 10 cores and 12 threads, and it can boost up to 5.2GHz, so it's a noticeable upgrade from its predecessor, and it should have better battery life, too. You also get up to 32GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD, more than enough for the use case of this laptop.

The 13.5-inch display is essentially the same as last year, and it borrows from the Spectre x360 above. It has a tall 3:2 aspect ratio, which is still pretty uncommon, and it comes in Full HD+ resolution by default, with the option for HP Sure View Reflect if you want a privacy screen. There's also an OLED panel option with 3000x2000 resolution, which will give you the best viewing experience. Above that display sits a 5MP webcam with Windows Hello facial recognition, still one of the best in any laptop.

Similarly, the design is basically unchanged from its predecessor, but the Dragonfly G4 still looks great. It weighs just 2.2 pounds, and it's 16.26mm thick, and it looks pretty great in its Slate Blue colorway (a silver option should also be available at some point). It also has a good supply of ports for a modern premium laptop, including two Thunderbolt 4 connections, USB Type-A, HDMI, a headphone jack, and a nano-SIM card slot for the cellular-enabled models. On that note, you can configure it with either 4G LTE or 5G connectivity, both powered by Intel modems.

The most notable downside of the HP Dragonfly G4 is that it costs a pretty penny outside of sales. That's pretty standard for business laptops, but it's important to keep an eye out for sales with this one if you want to get a good deal.

HP Spectre x360 16 (2023)
Best 15/16-inch laptop

Large display for productivity and creative work

$1200 $1700 Save $500

The 2023 HP Spectre x360 16 comes with upgraded Intel processors and optional Intel Arc graphics for workloads that require more GPU power. It has a super-sharp 16-inch display, including an optional OLED panel, too.

Pros
  • Powerful 13th-gen Intel processors and optional Intel Arc graphics
  • Very sharp 16-inch display, plus an OLED option
  • Beautiful dual-tone design
  • 5MP webcam
Cons
  • A little pricy
  • Somewhat heavy
$1200 at HP $1650 at Best Buy

If you want an HP laptop with a larger screen, you can't get much better than the HP Spectre x360 16. It's a powerful laptop with a versatile and striking design.

Speaking of power, the HP Spectre x360 16 comes with new 13th-generation Intel Core processors, and you can choose between a Core i7-13700H and a Core i7-1360P. If you choose the former, you get a more powerful CPU but no discrete graphics, while the latter option is paired with an Intel Arc A370M GPU if you need that kind of power. While the CPUs have been upgraded, the GPU is the same as last year's model. You can also configure the laptop with up to 32GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD.

The 16-inch panel comes in a 16:10 aspect ratio, and it's great in every configuration. The base model comes in a super sharp 3092x1920 resolution, but you can go even sharper with the 4K Ultra HD+ OLED model, which also has more vibrant colors and true blacks, typical of OLED. Those are the same options available last year, and the 5MP webcam is the same too, but it's still great, offering Windows Hello support, too.

In terms of design, the HP Spectre x360 16 hasn't changed much, and it's available in Nightfall Black or Nocturne Blue colors, sporting a dual-tone look similar to the 13.5-inch model. Due to the larger size, it's obviously heavier, coming in at 4.74 pounds, which makes this a little less portable. For ports, you get two Thunderbolt 4 connections, USB Type-A, HDMI, and a headphone jack, so there's solid connectivity overall.

All in all, this is an absolutely stellar laptop, and while it is a bit pricey, you definitely get your money's worth with it. It's not just one of HP's best laptops, it's one of the best laptops in general.

HP Envy 17 (2023)
Best 17-inch laptop

For those who want really large screens

$650 $1050 Save $400

The HP Envy 17 is a premium 17-inch laptop with an all-aluminum build and high-end specs, including 13th-generation Intel Core processors and optional Nvidia GeForce graphics. The large screen is ideal for multitasking, especially if you opt for the super-sharp 4K panel.

Pros
  • Large 17-inch display is great for productivity
  • Fast 13th-generation Intel CPUs and optional Nvidia graphics
  • 5MP webcam with auto frame and Windows Hello
Cons
  • You can only get Nvidia graphics with a 15W CPU
  • Pretty heavy
$650 at HP $1350 at Best Buy $849 at B&H

Need something a little bigger? Look no further than the fantastic HP Envy 17, a laptop with a large 17-inch display and enough power for all your day-to-day tasks, wrapped in a premium aluminum build.

The HP Envy 17 can be had with either an Intel Core i7-13700H or a Core i7-1355U processor. The former is faster but uses more power, while the latter is more power-efficient, but the U-series model comes with an Nvidia GeForce RTX 3050 Laptop GPU, giving you a big boost in performance for things like video editing and gaming. The GPU isn't the fastest around, but it should be able to run a lot of games if you don't mind tweaking some settings. You can also get up to 32GB of RAM and a 1TB SSD.

The display is a 17.3-inch panel, and the base configuration comes in Full HD resolution and a 16:9 aspect ratio. You can upgrade by adding touch support or upgrade to a 4K display, which makes a lot of sense for this large display. Above the display, we're looking at a 5MP webcam with all the smart features we've mentioned before, plus Windows Hello support.

Being this is a laptop with a large screen and a solid aluminum build, it does add up to a fairly heavy machine, weighing around 5.5 pounds. However, you do get a great selection of ports, including two Thunderbolt 4 connections, three USB Type-A ports, HDMI, a headphone jack, and a microSD card reader. The large size also makes room for a number pad on the side. A potential downside of the Envy 17 is that it only comes in silver, so it's not a very unique-looking machine.

If you want a large screen and a premium experience, the HP Envy 17 is the best option in HP's lineup and one of the best in general.

HP Envy 16 (2023)
Best laptop for creators

Powerful performance in a sleek design

$1199 $12991510 Save $12990311

The HP Envy 16 comes with up to a powerful Intel Core i9 processor and Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 graphics, making it ideal for demanding creator workloads and even some gaming, while keeping a sleek design. It even includes a stunning OLED display with a dynamic refresh rate up to 120Hz.

Pros
  • Powerful Intel CPUs and Nvidia RTX 40 series graphics
  • 16:10 display with stunning OLED 120Hz option
  • Premium and clean design
Cons
  • A bit on the heavy side
  • Looks may be a bit boring
$1550 at HP $1750 at Best Buy $1199 at B&H

Content creators need a lot of power, but gaming laptops can be heavy and garish. If you want that balance of power and style, the HP Envy 16 is great for you.

For starters, the laptop is powered by up to an Intel Core i9-13900H processor, meaning you get up to 14 cores and 20 threads, along with speeds up to 5.4GHz. For graphics, you get up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 Laptop GPU, which gives you plenty of power for video editing and gaming. Additionally, you can configure the laptop with up to 32GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD for storage.

Display-wise, the base model comes with a Quad HD+ panel with a 120Hz refresh rate, which can be upgraded with touch support if you have use for it. However, even more interesting is the 2.8K OLED touch-enabled option, which is even sharper and has a dynamic refresh rate that can adjust between 48Hz and 120Hz. Above that display, there's a 5MP webcam with smart features and Windows Hello support, a standard for HP's premium laptops since 2022.

HP's design language doesn't change often, and this is another silver laptop made from aluminum, so it looks and feels premium, but it's arguably too subdued. Because of the premium build quality and powerful specs, it's a somewhat heavy machine at 5.17 pounds. On the other hand, the ports are very good. With two Thunderbolt 4 and two USB Type-A ports, HDMI, a headphone jack, and a microSD card reader, you have plenty of options for connecting peripherals to your laptop. You can bypass docking stations with all the built-in ports, but having Thunderbolt does give you expansion options if you need them.

For what it offers, the HP Envy 16 isn't overly expensive, and it's an easy recommendation.

HP Pavilion Aero 13 (2023)
Best affordable lightweight laptop

Lightweight and capable at a reasonable price

$580 $880 Save $300

If you want a lightweight laptop that isn't overly expensive, the HP Pavilion Aero 13 is a fantastic choice. With AMD Ryzen 7000 series processors and a 16:10 display, you're getting great performance and multitasking capabilities.

Pros
  • Powerful AMD Ryzen 7035 series processors
  • Tall 16:10 display with optional Quad HD+ resolution
  • It weighs under 2.2 pounds and comes in four colors
Cons
  • 720p webcam
  • Newer models have been rising in price
$580 at HP $930 at HP (customizable) $800 at Amazon

If the laptops above are a little too expensive for you, the HP Pavilion Aero 13 is a fantastic option that's more affordable while still being very capable and surprisingly lightweight.

The latest model comes with powerful AMD Ryzen 7035 processors, up to a Ryzen 7 7735U with 8 cores and 16 threads. It offers really strong performance, and it's a major upgrade from previous models, especially in terms of integrated graphics, which are good enough for light gaming now. Combined with up to 16GB of RAM and a 1TB SSD, it makes for a great experience.

HP hasn't made many changes to the display, so we're looking at a 13.3-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio. The base model comes in Full HD+ resolution, but you can upgrade to a Quad HD+ panel, which makes this an excellent choice at this price point. The downside here is the webcam, which is still a 720p sensor without facial recognition, a clear step down from HP's more premium laptops. There's a fingerprint reader for Windows Hello, at least.

Another great thing about this laptop is the design. For starters, it weighs just under 2.2 pounds, which is super light for such a cheap laptop, and that's on top of coming in four colors to choose from. It has a fairly basic design, but these color options make help it stand out. And for ports, you get one USB Type-C and two Type-A ports, HDMI, and a headphone jack, which is a solid setup.

HP has been increasing the price of the Pavilion Aero over the past couple of years, but this is still a great and relatively affordable laptop. Sales have been frequent with past models, so it's worth keeping an eye out if you're interested in saving more money. We reviewed the original model back in 2021 if you'd like to know more.

HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook
Best Chromebook

Powerful processors and a stunning display

HP makes some of the best Chromebooks around, and the Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is no exception. With top-tier Intel processors, a bright display, and even some RGB lighting, this is an exceptional machine if you want a Chromebook. Unfortunately, it's pricier than most Chrome OS laptops.

Pros
  • 12th-generation Intel CPUs delivering fantastic performance on Chrome OS
  • Sharp Quad HD+ display with a whopping 1,200 nits of brightness
  • 8MP webcam
Cons
  • A little heavier than it could be
  • Very expensive for a Chromebook
$1000 at HP

HP has been going strong with its Chromebooks, and the latest Dragonfly Pro Chromebook, despite the odd naming convention, is a great example of why. It's a fantastic machine in almost every way, with only a couple of notable downsides.

For starters, performance is handled by a 12th-generation Intel Core i5-1235U, a processor with 10 cores and 12 threads that can boost up to 4.4GHz. It's not the cream of the crop anymore, but it's far from a slow processor, and more importantly, it's perfect for ChromeOS, which is a much lighter operating system than Windows. The only configuration available also includes 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD, rounding out a great experience.

One of the highlights of this laptop is the display, which is a 14-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio and super-sharp Quad HD+ resolution. That's already great, but what's even more special is that this display can reach an impressive 1,200 nits of brightness, making it easily visible just about anywhere. Likewise, the webcam is one of the best you'll see on any laptop thanks to the 8MP sensor. ChromeOS doesn't support facial recognition for login, but the laptop has a fingerprint reader instead.

As far as the design goes, the HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook looks great in both Ceramic White and Sparkling Black, and it even has a cool RGB-backlit keyboard to give it a bit of extra flair. One downside is that it's a little heavy considering the size, coming in at 3.33 pounds. It's not super heavy, but not as portable as it could be. It also doesn't have a ton of ports, though it does include a whopping four Thunderbolt 4 connections. However, it lacks things like a headphone jack, so you may need some adapters.

Despite some design blunders, the HP Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is a fantastic laptop. Aside from being a little heavy, the biggest downside is that it's expensive, coming in at $1,000. However, it's easily the best ChromeOS experience the company offers.

HP Chromebook Plus 15a
Best affordable Chromebook

Solid performance and premium design at a great price

$349 $499 Save $150

Despite the affordable price tag, the HP Chromebook Plus 15a is a very capable ChromeOS laptop, featuring an 8-core processor, along with 8GB of RAM and 128GB of UFS storage. It also has a large Full HD display and a premium design using recycled aluminum.

Pros
  • 8-core Intel processor delivers solid performance for everyday use
  • Large Full HD display isn't always a guarantee at this price
  • Sleek design with recycled aluminum for a premium feel
Cons
  • Not the lightest laptop around
$349 at Best Buy $430 at HP

HP makes a ton of Chromebooks, and if the Dragonfly Pro Chromebook is too expensive for you, the recently-launched HP Chromebook Plus 15a is an excellent alternative.

Inside this laptop is an Intel Core i3-N305 processor, which is a low-power CPU with eight cores and eight threads. That's more than enough for solid performance in day-to-day tasks, and it comes, especially with a lightweight platform like ChromeOS. Plus, it has 8GB of RAM and 128GB of UFS storage, so it has great performance for the price.

The display is solid, too. At this price range, it's not always guaranteed you'll get a Full HD panel, especially on a 15-inch laptop, but that's exactly what you get here, which is great to see. What's even rarer is a 1080p webcam, but that's included, too, so it's really hard to complain about what you're getting at this price.

Coming around to the design, the laptop features a sleek silver look with a chassis that uses recycled aluminum and plastic for a laptop that still feels premium despite its low price. It weighs 3.81 pounds, which is reasonable for a 15-inch laptop. As for ports, it has two USB-C and one USB Type-A, plus a headphone jack and a microSD card reader.

For $500, this is a very good laptop, but if you can find it on sale (as it is at writing time), then it's an absolute steal.

Best HP laptops in 2023: Bottom line

These are the best laptops you can get from HP right now. Whether it's a gift for a loved one or for yourself, these are all fantastic options for different types of users. For most people, the HP Spectre x360 13.5 is likely the best because it's got it all: Great performance, a stunning display that's even better with the OLED model, and a great webcam, all packed in a beautiful premium design that's not overly heavy.

HP Spectre x360 13.5 (2023)
Best overall
$900 $1500 Save $600

The 2023 HP Spectre x360 13.5 packs 13th-generation Intel Core processors in a sleek and stylish design. It also has a great 13.5-inch display with a 3:2 aspect ratio that makes it optimal for productivity.

$900 at Best Buy

But of course, there's something for everyone, and if your budget is a little tighter, it's always worth looking at the Pavilion Aero, which offers absolutely stellar value for its price. It is a bit more expensive than its predecessors, but this year's model is also significantly more powerful thanks to AMD's Ryzen 7035 series processor, while still having a great display and a very lightweight design. If you want to save even more and you don't need anything fancy, you can go with the HP Chromebook x360 13.3, too.

We included a couple of cellular-connected laptops on this list, but if you're looking for more, check out our list of the best 5G laptops you can buy today. There are plenty of great options there too.

item.title: 3 reasons Apple will try to revive Mac gaming at the 'Scary fast' event
item.itunes:title: 3 reasons Apple will try to revive Mac gaming at the 'Scary fast' event
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Key Takeaways:
  • Apple has significantly improved the power and performance of its Macs, making them more capable of handling demanding AAA games.
  • Apple has already prioritized gaming with the introduction of hardware-accelerated ray tracing on the iPhone 15 Pro, and plans to bring AAA titles to Mac.
  • The macOS Sonoma release included gaming-related features such as Game Mode and the Game Porting Toolkit, showing Apple's strong investment in Mac gaming.

In case you missed it, Apple is hosting a special event for the Mac next week. It has been dubbed 'Scary fast' by the company, which gives each event a short teaser name to hint at what's to come. There's a lot that Apple could do at this event, and we've rounded up all the possibilities in a great event preview. But amidst all the rumors and leaks we've seen ahead of 'Scary fast,' my bet is that Apple will give its best shot at trying to revive Mac gaming at the event. If you look closely at Apple's recent moves, all signs point to gaming. If you aren't convinced yet, here are three reasons why Apple will emphasize gaming at its Oct. 30 event.

1 There's finally enough power to make it worthwhile

As much as we talk about software and game developer support when it comes to Mac gaming, an undeniable part of the conversation is hardware. To put it simply, the best Macs from a few years ago are exponentially underpowered compared to the best Macs today. Intel and AMD-equipped Macs released in the late 2010s just weren't up to the task of playing demanding AAA titles. These machines had thermal issues, some models had meager specs, and limited to no hardware upgrade paths. Just about the only systems that could handle intensive gaming were the highest-end models, like the 'trash can' Mac Pro, and those computers had problems of their own.

While Apple hasn't done anything in the way of hardware upgradeability, it has fixed the other two concerns with Apple Silicon. We're expecting to see an M3 system-on-a-chip platform debut at 'Scary Fast' built on the 3nm process. This should improve on the advancements of M1 and M2, which are already some of the best chips around in terms of power and efficiency. All of this means that it's the perfect time for Apple to give Mac gaming another try. It has fixed the hardware, boasting impressive performance and great thermal thresholds. Now it just has to get gamers and developers onboard.

2 Apple already placed emphasis on A17 Pro gaming

Perhaps the biggest indicator that Apple plans to try and jumpstart Mac gaming is that it has made gaming a focal point of the iPhone 15 Pro series. With the new A17 Pro chip, Apple is adding hardware-accelerated ray tracing to the iPhone for the first time. It's a huge addition, and Apple has already committed to bringing AAA titles like Assassin's Creed: Mirage to the iPhone. But this move has much bigger implications for macOS gaming than you might think.

If you recall, when Apple brought Apple Silicon to the Mac, it added the ability for macOS to open apps designed for iPad and iPhone operating systems. Essentially, this means that developers can create a single app for all platforms. Apple recently confirmed in an interview with IGN what was already my suspicion: these AAA titles coming to the iPhone will also come to iPad and Mac. "The developers are going to work with us to do it," said Tim Millet, Apple’s vice president of platform architecture.

This doesn't necessarily mean that we'll see previews of game ports on macOS at the 'Scary Fast' event. But it does reinforce that Apple now views gaming as part of its product and software visions.

3 A big part of macOS Sonoma was gaming-related

If you weren't looking for it in the macOS Sonoma release, it might have flown under the radar. But make no mistake: a massive part of this yearly OS release was devoted to gaming. Apple added gaming-related features both on the development side (Game Porting Toolkit) and the consumer side (Game Mode) in this release, making macOS Sonoma a well-rounded release. Starting with the simple one first, Game Mode is designed to prioritize gaming performance when you're playing a full-screen game, limiting background activity. I took a look at how exactly Game Mode works earlier this month, and it's very effective.

But Game Mode doesn't matter as much if there aren't high-quality games available on the Mac. That's where the Game Porting Toolkit comes into play. It allows developers to easily port their games to the Mac, simplifying the translation layer for a game's shading and graphics code. Our Lead Technical Editor tried this out for himself, and was impressed by how playing Cyberpunk 2077 and Spider-Man: Remastered turned out. While anyone with an Apple Developer account can use the toolkit, it's designed for game developers. Apple put time into creating both of these macOS Sonoma features, which implies it is now very invested in Mac gaming.

Final thoughts

When Apple makes a move as significant as going all-in on gaming would be, it usually tries to be consistent across its entire ecosystem. That's Apple's whole pitch, after all. The ecosystem is designed to just work across the company's entire portfolio of products, from iPhone to Mac. What we've seen over the past few months is a deliberate effort to prepare for gaming to become a much bigger part of Apple's plan than it has been in the past. Now that the 'Scary Fast' event is here, I think we'll see something that ties it all together.

item.title: Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB SSD review: Leading the second generation of PCIe 5.0 SSDs
item.itunes:title: Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB SSD review: Leading the second generation of PCIe 5.0 SSDs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The race to make the best and fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD is continuing to heat up this year. We've already blown through the 10,000MB/s generation of SSDs, and we're now firmly in the 12,000MB/s generation as the first drives capable of these speeds are coming out. One of these drives is Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB, which claims 12,000MB/s in both reads and writes, which would put it on the board as one of the fastest SSDs, if not the fastest.

The Z540 2TB isn't just a drive capable of 12,000MB/s, it may very well be the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now thanks to its great overall performance and excellent peak performance. At least until 14,000MB/s drives start coming out (which isn't far off), the Z540 will be a great choice for anyone seeking the fastest storage possible.

About this review: Teamgroup sent me the T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB for the purposes of this review. Teamgroup did not see the contents of this review before publishing.

Source: Teamgroup
Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540
A top-end SSD

The Z540 is the champion of PCIe 5.0 SSDs, for now

9 / 10
$260 $300 Save $40

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is a high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD that can hit 12GB/s in reads and writes. It comes in either 1TB or 2TB sizes.

Pros
  • Top-end PCIe 5.0 performance
  • Endurance on par with good PCIe 5.0 drives
  • Competitive price
Cons
  • Hot and requires passive or active cooling
  • Only 1TB and 2TB capacities
$260 at Amazon $260 at Newegg

Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540: Pricing and availability

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 has just launched on Amazon, and it seems it will only be available in 1TB and 2TB sizes, which cost $150 and $260 respectively. Other 12,000MB/s drives are similarly priced. Both Gigabyte's Aorus Gen5 12000 and Crucial's T700 generally cost $160 for the 1TB model and $270 for the 2TB. As for 10,000MB/s SSDs, Seagate's FireCuda 540 is $180 for 1TB and $300 for 2TB, and MSI's Spatium M570 (which is only available in 2TB right now) is $270, which are also similar to the Z540 and its rivals.

The only area where the Z540 is lagging is that it doesn't offer a 4TB model like the T700. However, the T700 is also the only PCIe 5.0 SSD thus far to offer any capacity bigger than 2TB, so the Z540 is really only lacking against the T700 specifically rather than a wider array of SSDs.

How the Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540 was tested

For benchmarks, I used my Intel test system that uses the Core i9-14900K, Z790 Taichi Lite, and 32GB of DDR5 clocked at 5,600MHz and running at CL40 timings. Generally speaking, PCs using Intel chips have the best chance of showing the best possible performance on PCIe 5.0 SSDs, though the difference may not be all that great, and PCIe 5.0 SSDs will still be very fast in AMD-powered PCs. Software-wise, I used Windows 11, which was up-to-date as of Oct. 17, as were all other apps and drivers.

I've run the Z540 and three of its competitors through a series of benchmarks: CrystalDiskMark, 3DMark's storage test, and IOMeter. These tests will give us a good idea of best-case-scenario performance, gaming performance, and long-term writing performance, which are the three key areas of interest for an SSD. As I was benchmarking, I was very carefully to not allow depleted SSD cache to impact the results, so between every test I waited at least 10 minutes for the cache to replenish.

Teamgroup also sent its T-Force Dark Airflow I SSD heatsink with active cooling, which I didn't use for these benchmarks but did test separately. It will be available around the same time the Z540 launches as a separate product.

Performance

In CrystalDiskMark, I used the six tests the program comes with by default. Half of these tests measure sequential performance and the other half measure random performance, and these individual tests also differ in other parameters like queue depth, block size, and thread count.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

SEQ1M Q8T1

12,391/11,701

11,682/9,537

10,073/10,197

10,081/10,197

SEQ1M Q1T1

9,297/9,636

9,169/9,216

8,601/9,622

8,631/9,610

SEQ128K Q32T1

12,281/11,479

11,471/9,563

9,778/10,151

9,933/10,187

RND4K Q32T16

6,382/7,031

5,697/6,536

6,106/6,797

6,109/6,667

RND4K Q32T1

1,169/858

1,178/851

1,155/839

1,161/854

RND4K Q1T1

101/393

101/397

100/385

99/397

Scores are organized by read/write and are measured in MB/s.

In all of the CrystalDiskMark tests, the Z540 2TB is either the winner or tied for first. All four drives are pretty close in the random tests especially, which isn't surprising since random performance doesn't really benefit from the greater maximum bandwidth that PCIe 5.0 provides. It's really in the sequential benchmarks where the Z540 shines, beating all drives by fairly large margins in both reads and writes.

If you're after the fastest peak speeds on an SSD, the Z540 2TB is what you want.

However, one thing I will point out is that the Gen5 12000 2TB would probably be just about as fast as the Z540 2TB. On many drives, performance is correlated with capacity (at least up to a certain point), and the 1TB variants of both the Z540 and Gen5 12000 are significantly slower than their 2TB counterparts. The Gen5 12000 1TB's performance is mostly there to just show that getting a 1TB PCIe 5.0 SSD probably isn't a great idea.

3DMark's storage test actually runs real workloads on real games, like Overwatch and Battlefield V. It measures both speed and latency on SSDs, both of which are important for load times in games. More points means more performance.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

Score

5,783

5,772

5,620

5,423

The Z540 barely scores a victory here, but the margin is so thin I'd put it on par with the Gen5 12000 1TB. It is however a bit ahead of the FireCuda 540 2TB and the Spatium M570 2TB as well. We're probably approaching the point where the SSD test in 3DMark won't return much higher results for drives that are clearly better, as it's just too lightweight and tests old games; some sort of DirectStorage benchmark would be nice to see in the future.

The Z540 2TB isn't just a drive capable of 12,000MB/s, it may very well be the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now thanks to its great overall performance and excellent peak performance.

Finally, we have IOMeter, which is an old-school Intel app that I use to test SSDs over the course of a 15-minute sequential writing workload. The point in doing this is to test the long-term writing performance of these SSDs, which can easily decrease even after just a few minutes. All modern SSDs have a small amount of storage that is configured to be faster than the rest, and this will get depleted after continuous writing. Additionally, SSDs get slower the more they're filled up, so performance with half an SSD filled will be slower (sometimes much slower) than if it were almost completely empty.

In this first benchmark I've set up each drive to be half full, and then ran IOMeter for 15 minutes.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

Average Write Speed

3,613

2,082

3,816

3,182

Scores are measured in MB/s.

For about the first minute of the test, the Z540 2TB is just barely below 12,000MB/s, while the FireCuda 540 and Spatium M570 are at 10,000MB/s, and the Gen5 12000 is actually running just over 9,000MB/s. For the next four minutes, the FireCuda 540 and Spatium M570 are significantly ahead of the Z540 and the Gen5 12000, though after the five-minute mark the Z540 recovers and ends up averaging 3,613MB/s, just behind the FireCuda 540 at 3,816MB/s and well ahead of the other two SSDs.

Active cooling makes a big difference for the Z540 and basically any other current PCIe 5.0 SSD in sustained workloads.

In addition to running IOMeter with the SSDs halfway full, I also tested IOMeter with the drives filled to 10% and 90%. In the chart below, I've only included the Z540 for simplicity, but the table below offers performance data for the other three SSDs.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

10% full

9,329

8,262

9,000

6,388

50% full

3,609

2,082

3,812

3,182

90% full

2,721

1,912

3,583

2,999

Scores are measured in MB/s.

Although the Z540 2TB's performance at 10% full looks messy, on average it's quite good at 9,329MB/s, which makes it the fastest among these drives. At 50% full, the FireCuda 540 2TB takes a small lead, though the Z540 2TB is still faster than the other two SSDs. Once at 90% full, the Z540 2TB falls behind even the Spatium M570 and the FireCuda 540 furthers its lead. While this is a bit of a mixed bag for the Z540, generally speaking you don't even want to fill up your main drive completely so that you can avoid this kind of performance degradation entirely.

One last thing to note here is thermals and if the T-Force Dark Airflow I SSD cooler actually makes a difference. PCIe 5.0 SSDs tend to get pretty hot, and high temperatures can cause thermal throttling, which reduces performance. However, because filling up a drive also reduces performance and does so by quite a large amount, thermal throttling is really only a performance concern for an SSD that's not filled that much and can reach peak performance.

In the IOMeter 10% filled test, the Z540 hit a peak 81 C around the 3-minute mark under the Taichi Lite's passive heatsink, while it only got as hot as 78 C under the Dark Airflow cooler after about 14 minutes. But while this temperature difference is small, the performance difference was massive. Under the passive cooler, the Z540 averaged 9,329MB/s, but the active cooler boosted this up to 11,443MB/s, close to the drive's peak performance.

Active cooling makes a big difference for the Z540 and basically any other current PCIe 5.0 SSD in sustained workloads, which means there's a bit of a dilemma for users: whether to use passive or active cooling. Passive cooling is convenient and silent, but doesn't offer peak performance in long writing operations. Active cooling will however get the Z540 and other SSDs to perform much better, but it can be loud and take up significant room, and it also requires a separate purchase. Even without a heatsink though, the Z540 is fine, as thermals will only matter in a minutes-long file transfer.

Should you buy the Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540?

You should buy the TeamGroup T-Force Cardea Z540 if:

  • You want high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD performance
  • You want a decent deal for the capacity and speed
  • You don't plan on filling your main SSD up very much

You shouldn't buy the TeamGroup T-Force Cardea Z540 if:

  • You can wait for faster 14,000MB/s drives coming out soon
  • You need an SSD with better value per gigabyte
  • You need storage capacity more than performance

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB either won or tied in pretty much every single benchmark, except perhaps for IOMeter if you're just looking at higher fill rate performance. If you're after the fastest peak speeds on an SSD, the Z540 2TB is what you want (plus an active cooler like Teamgroup's T-Force Dark Airflow I). The Z540 2TB also has good sustain writing performance and endurance typical for a PCIe 5.0 drive at 1,400TBW. Plus, it's either priced similarly as its rivals or cheaper, making the Z540 the obvious choice for most people.

It's really in the sequential benchmarks where the Z540 shines.

However, don't make the mistake of getting the 1TB model of the Z540, and although I didn't test the 1TB model, the performance of Gigabyte's Aorus Gen5 12000 1TB is probably what you'd get with the Z540 1TB. Neither the performance nor the capacity is good for the price, and if you're in the market for a PCIe 5.0 SSD, you probably have the budget for a 2TB drive and won't need to save money by trying to buy a 1TB model.

Things are moving fast for PCIe 5.0 SSDs however. Earlier this year, the first generation of PCIe 5.0 drives launched with speeds of 10,000MB/s, but it was only a few months later around the middle of the year when the first 12,000MB/s drives started hitting the shelves. Engineering samples of 14,000MB/s SSDs already exist and they're bound to come out soon, perhaps early next year, so the Z540 won't offer top-end performance forever. Regardless, the Z540 a great SSD, and I expect it should still be worth buying even when we enter the 14,000MB/s generation of PCIe 5.0 SSDs.

Source: Teamgroup
Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540
One of the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSDs
$260 $300 Save $40

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is a high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD that can hit 12GB/s in reads and writes. It comes in either 1TB or 2TB sizes.

$260 at Amazon $260 at Newegg

We've already blown through the 10,000MB/s generation of SSDs, and we're now firmly in the 12,000MB/s generation as the first drives capable of these speeds are coming out. One of these drives is Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB, which claims 12,000MB/s in both reads and writes, which would put it on the board as one of the fastest SSDs, if not the fastest.

The Z540 2TB isn't just a drive capable of 12,000MB/s, it may very well be the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now thanks to its great overall performance and excellent peak performance. At least until 14,000MB/s drives start coming out (which isn't far off), the Z540 will be a great choice for anyone seeking the fastest storage possible.

About this review: Teamgroup sent me the T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB for the purposes of this review. Teamgroup did not see the contents of this review before publishing.

Source: Teamgroup
Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540
A top-end SSD

The Z540 is the champion of PCIe 5.0 SSDs, for now

9 / 10
$260 $300 Save $40

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is a high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD that can hit 12GB/s in reads and writes. It comes in either 1TB or 2TB sizes.

Pros
  • Top-end PCIe 5.0 performance
  • Endurance on par with good PCIe 5.0 drives
  • Competitive price
Cons
  • Hot and requires passive or active cooling
  • Only 1TB and 2TB capacities
$260 at Amazon $260 at Newegg

Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540: Pricing and availability

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 has just launched on Amazon, and it seems it will only be available in 1TB and 2TB sizes, which cost $150 and $260 respectively. Other 12,000MB/s drives are similarly priced. Both Gigabyte's Aorus Gen5 12000 and Crucial's T700 generally cost $160 for the 1TB model and $270 for the 2TB. As for 10,000MB/s SSDs, Seagate's FireCuda 540 is $180 for 1TB and $300 for 2TB, and MSI's Spatium M570 (which is only available in 2TB right now) is $270, which are also similar to the Z540 and its rivals.

The only area where the Z540 is lagging is that it doesn't offer a 4TB model like the T700. However, the T700 is also the only PCIe 5.0 SSD thus far to offer any capacity bigger than 2TB, so the Z540 is really only lacking against the T700 specifically rather than a wider array of SSDs.

How the Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540 was tested

For benchmarks, I used my Intel test system that uses the Core i9-14900K, Z790 Taichi Lite, and 32GB of DDR5 clocked at 5,600MHz and running at CL40 timings. Generally speaking, PCs using Intel chips have the best chance of showing the best possible performance on PCIe 5.0 SSDs, though the difference may not be all that great, and PCIe 5.0 SSDs will still be very fast in AMD-powered PCs. Software-wise, I used Windows 11, which was up-to-date as of Oct. 17, as were all other apps and drivers.

I've run the Z540 and three of its competitors through a series of benchmarks: CrystalDiskMark, 3DMark's storage test, and IOMeter. These tests will give us a good idea of best-case-scenario performance, gaming performance, and long-term writing performance, which are the three key areas of interest for an SSD. As I was benchmarking, I was very carefully to not allow depleted SSD cache to impact the results, so between every test I waited at least 10 minutes for the cache to replenish.

Teamgroup also sent its T-Force Dark Airflow I SSD heatsink with active cooling, which I didn't use for these benchmarks but did test separately. It will be available around the same time the Z540 launches as a separate product.

Performance

In CrystalDiskMark, I used the six tests the program comes with by default. Half of these tests measure sequential performance and the other half measure random performance, and these individual tests also differ in other parameters like queue depth, block size, and thread count.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

SEQ1M Q8T1

12,391/11,701

11,682/9,537

10,073/10,197

10,081/10,197

SEQ1M Q1T1

9,297/9,636

9,169/9,216

8,601/9,622

8,631/9,610

SEQ128K Q32T1

12,281/11,479

11,471/9,563

9,778/10,151

9,933/10,187

RND4K Q32T16

6,382/7,031

5,697/6,536

6,106/6,797

6,109/6,667

RND4K Q32T1

1,169/858

1,178/851

1,155/839

1,161/854

RND4K Q1T1

101/393

101/397

100/385

99/397

Scores are organized by read/write and are measured in MB/s.

In all of the CrystalDiskMark tests, the Z540 2TB is either the winner or tied for first. All four drives are pretty close in the random tests especially, which isn't surprising since random performance doesn't really benefit from the greater maximum bandwidth that PCIe 5.0 provides. It's really in the sequential benchmarks where the Z540 shines, beating all drives by fairly large margins in both reads and writes.

If you're after the fastest peak speeds on an SSD, the Z540 2TB is what you want.

However, one thing I will point out is that the Gen5 12000 2TB would probably be just about as fast as the Z540 2TB. On many drives, performance is correlated with capacity (at least up to a certain point), and the 1TB variants of both the Z540 and Gen5 12000 are significantly slower than their 2TB counterparts. The Gen5 12000 1TB's performance is mostly there to just show that getting a 1TB PCIe 5.0 SSD probably isn't a great idea.

3DMark's storage test actually runs real workloads on real games, like Overwatch and Battlefield V. It measures both speed and latency on SSDs, both of which are important for load times in games. More points means more performance.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

Score

5,783

5,772

5,620

5,423

The Z540 barely scores a victory here, but the margin is so thin I'd put it on par with the Gen5 12000 1TB. It is however a bit ahead of the FireCuda 540 2TB and the Spatium M570 2TB as well. We're probably approaching the point where the SSD test in 3DMark won't return much higher results for drives that are clearly better, as it's just too lightweight and tests old games; some sort of DirectStorage benchmark would be nice to see in the future.

The Z540 2TB isn't just a drive capable of 12,000MB/s, it may very well be the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now thanks to its great overall performance and excellent peak performance.

Finally, we have IOMeter, which is an old-school Intel app that I use to test SSDs over the course of a 15-minute sequential writing workload. The point in doing this is to test the long-term writing performance of these SSDs, which can easily decrease even after just a few minutes. All modern SSDs have a small amount of storage that is configured to be faster than the rest, and this will get depleted after continuous writing. Additionally, SSDs get slower the more they're filled up, so performance with half an SSD filled will be slower (sometimes much slower) than if it were almost completely empty.

In this first benchmark I've set up each drive to be half full, and then ran IOMeter for 15 minutes.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

Average Write Speed

3,613

2,082

3,816

3,182

Scores are measured in MB/s.

For about the first minute of the test, the Z540 2TB is just barely below 12,000MB/s, while the FireCuda 540 and Spatium M570 are at 10,000MB/s, and the Gen5 12000 is actually running just over 9,000MB/s. For the next four minutes, the FireCuda 540 and Spatium M570 are significantly ahead of the Z540 and the Gen5 12000, though after the five-minute mark the Z540 recovers and ends up averaging 3,613MB/s, just behind the FireCuda 540 at 3,816MB/s and well ahead of the other two SSDs.

Active cooling makes a big difference for the Z540 and basically any other current PCIe 5.0 SSD in sustained workloads.

In addition to running IOMeter with the SSDs halfway full, I also tested IOMeter with the drives filled to 10% and 90%. In the chart below, I've only included the Z540 for simplicity, but the table below offers performance data for the other three SSDs.

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Spatium M570 2TB

10% full

9,329

8,262

9,000

6,388

50% full

3,609

2,082

3,812

3,182

90% full

2,721

1,912

3,583

2,999

Scores are measured in MB/s.

Although the Z540 2TB's performance at 10% full looks messy, on average it's quite good at 9,329MB/s, which makes it the fastest among these drives. At 50% full, the FireCuda 540 2TB takes a small lead, though the Z540 2TB is still faster than the other two SSDs. Once at 90% full, the Z540 2TB falls behind even the Spatium M570 and the FireCuda 540 furthers its lead. While this is a bit of a mixed bag for the Z540, generally speaking you don't even want to fill up your main drive completely so that you can avoid this kind of performance degradation entirely.

One last thing to note here is thermals and if the T-Force Dark Airflow I SSD cooler actually makes a difference. PCIe 5.0 SSDs tend to get pretty hot, and high temperatures can cause thermal throttling, which reduces performance. However, because filling up a drive also reduces performance and does so by quite a large amount, thermal throttling is really only a performance concern for an SSD that's not filled that much and can reach peak performance.

In the IOMeter 10% filled test, the Z540 hit a peak 81 C around the 3-minute mark under the Taichi Lite's passive heatsink, while it only got as hot as 78 C under the Dark Airflow cooler after about 14 minutes. But while this temperature difference is small, the performance difference was massive. Under the passive cooler, the Z540 averaged 9,329MB/s, but the active cooler boosted this up to 11,443MB/s, close to the drive's peak performance.

Active cooling makes a big difference for the Z540 and basically any other current PCIe 5.0 SSD in sustained workloads, which means there's a bit of a dilemma for users: whether to use passive or active cooling. Passive cooling is convenient and silent, but doesn't offer peak performance in long writing operations. Active cooling will however get the Z540 and other SSDs to perform much better, but it can be loud and take up significant room, and it also requires a separate purchase. Even without a heatsink though, the Z540 is fine, as thermals will only matter in a minutes-long file transfer.

Should you buy the Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540?

You should buy the TeamGroup T-Force Cardea Z540 if:

  • You want high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD performance
  • You want a decent deal for the capacity and speed
  • You don't plan on filling your main SSD up very much

You shouldn't buy the TeamGroup T-Force Cardea Z540 if:

  • You can wait for faster 14,000MB/s drives coming out soon
  • You need an SSD with better value per gigabyte
  • You need storage capacity more than performance

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 2TB either won or tied in pretty much every single benchmark, except perhaps for IOMeter if you're just looking at higher fill rate performance. If you're after the fastest peak speeds on an SSD, the Z540 2TB is what you want (plus an active cooler like Teamgroup's T-Force Dark Airflow I). The Z540 2TB also has good sustain writing performance and endurance typical for a PCIe 5.0 drive at 1,400TBW. Plus, it's either priced similarly as its rivals or cheaper, making the Z540 the obvious choice for most people.

It's really in the sequential benchmarks where the Z540 shines.

However, don't make the mistake of getting the 1TB model of the Z540, and although I didn't test the 1TB model, the performance of Gigabyte's Aorus Gen5 12000 1TB is probably what you'd get with the Z540 1TB. Neither the performance nor the capacity is good for the price, and if you're in the market for a PCIe 5.0 SSD, you probably have the budget for a 2TB drive and won't need to save money by trying to buy a 1TB model.

Things are moving fast for PCIe 5.0 SSDs however. Earlier this year, the first generation of PCIe 5.0 drives launched with speeds of 10,000MB/s, but it was only a few months later around the middle of the year when the first 12,000MB/s drives started hitting the shelves. Engineering samples of 14,000MB/s SSDs already exist and they're bound to come out soon, perhaps early next year, so the Z540 won't offer top-end performance forever. Regardless, the Z540 a great SSD, and I expect it should still be worth buying even when we enter the 14,000MB/s generation of PCIe 5.0 SSDs.

Source: Teamgroup
Teamgroup T-Force Cardea Z540
One of the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSDs
$260 $300 Save $40

Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is a high-end PCIe 5.0 SSD that can hit 12GB/s in reads and writes. It comes in either 1TB or 2TB sizes.

$260 at Amazon $260 at Newegg

item.title: These JBL wireless headphones hit record low price
item.itunes:title: These JBL wireless headphones hit record low price
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Source: JBL
JBL Tune 710BT
$40 $80 Save $40

These over-ear Bluetooth headphones from JBL feature a lightweight and foldable design, making them an excellent choice for travel. JBL Pure Bass Sound is baked in, favoring punchy, loud, deep tones. Battery life is 50 hours, and you can gain three more hours of listening with a quick five-minute charge. Tactile controls below the ear muffs give you access to volume, track skipping and playing, and help you make and take calls.

$40 at Amazon

JBL is a well-known brand in the headphones industry, offering both premium quality and mid-range options. And even though JBL Tune 710BT wireless headphones are of mid-range quality for over-ear headphones, you can get them at the price point of entry-level ones, thanks to a price drop at Amazon.

JBL Tune 710BT headphones are now available at a record-low price of 39.95 at Amazon, down from $79.95. That's a $40 discount, enough to buy cases for your phones from the best brands and still have money left to buy the best VPN service.

What's great about JBL Tune 710BT headphones

From sound quality to their design, there are plenty of things great about the JBL Tune 710BT headphones. They feature a foldable design, which means you can carry them with you easily. Not only that, but the headphones are comfortable to wear, thanks to the quality padding the pair have.

The Tune 710BT headphones have punchy, loud, deep tones, as it's signed by JBL Pure Bass. They feature Bluetooth 5.0, allowing you to pair them with two devices at the same time. They have a great battery life, lasting up to 50 hours on a single charge. The Tune 710BT headphones also come with fast-charging support, helping you get three hours of listening with five minutes of charging.

The JBL Tune 710BT headphones have a button on the right earcup, easily allowing you to control your sound, manage calls, and trigger the voice assistant. All of these goodies are available at a record-low price, so don't miss this rare opportunity to pick one pair for you or your loved ones while the deal lasts.

The Tune 710BT headphones also come with fast-charging support, helping you get three hours of listening with five minutes of charging.

The JBL Tune 710BT headphones have a button on the right earcup, easily allowing you to control your sound, manage calls, and trigger the voice assistant. All of these goodies are available at a record-low price, so don't miss this rare opportunity to pick one pair for you or your loved ones while the deal lasts.

item.title: Does CPU affect download speed?
item.itunes:title: Does CPU affect download speed?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For anybody who uses their computer for more than light internet searching, the last thing that you want is a computer that's lagging and slow. Whether it's with said internet searching, downloading and transferring files, uploading photos, or logging hours of gaming, the faster the better. You rely on your computer for so much and need it to be reliable, especially if you're relying on it for a lot of tasks. That's why the central processing unit, or CPU, is such an important component for a laptop or PC. The best CPUs are ones that boost your computer and handle tasks in an efficient and swift manner.

But just how important is a CPU to all the duties that your computer handles? If you're trying to find out exactly what the CPU can handle, it makes sense to find out what it does and what it doesn't do. If you're wondering if a CPU is affecting things like your download speed, there's more that needs explaining.

What does a CPU do?

The CPU is the unit that handles all the computation and runs the operating system. Whether you have Windows, macOS, or Linux, your CPU is the one running it. The CPU has cores and threads that carry out the tasks as fast as they can, and the better the CPU, the faster it is going to be carrying out the tasks. Certain CPUs, like ones meant for gaming laptops or PCs, are made to handle complex computations without slowing you down much. Some of the top processors that you're likely to see on the market today come from AMD, Intel, Qualcomm, and Apple. The CPU sits in the motherboard of the computer and interacts with drivers of other devices to make them run.

How are download speeds affected by CPUs?

In truth, CPUs don't affect download speeds. While the better a processor is, the faster the computer will handle tasks. If you have a newer processor, like a 13th-generation Intel processor or an M2 Max, they are built to take in a lot of information at once and make your computer move quickly while carrying out the task. The better processor can transfer information more quickly. But it won't affect how fast your download speed is.

Download speeds are measured in Megabits per second (Mbps). It measures the rate at which information is downloaded from the Internet to your computer. Downloads are categorized in bandwidth, which is the amount of information that your computer is either downloading or uploaded each second. The CPU doesn't directly affect bandwidth and download speeds. But there are plenty of other factors that affect download speeds.

So what does affect download speeds?

There are other internal factors that will affect how your computer runs. Your motherboard could potentially be affecting your download speeds. If you have a lot of bandwidth, but your motherboard isn't quite built to handle it all, upgrading it could help. This won't always be the case, as most motherboards are built to handle gigabit speeds. But the more bandwidth, the heavier task it is asking the motherboard to handle.

Another factor is your internet. If your connection speeds are slower, the download will download more slowly. If your network is congested with multiple tabs open or videos running, it can cause the downloads to take longer as well. Think about a traffic jam moving slowly simply because there are more cars that have to move through a given area.

The size of the file also matters. Smaller files will take less time to download, and larger files will take longer. So, if you're wondering why your computer is taking a long time to download a file, there could be multiple reasons for it, but the CPU isn't directly one of them.

item.title: Best Google Pixel Fold deals in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best Google Pixel Fold deals in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There are now more choices than ever in the United States when it comes to foldable smartphones. Although Samsung is still a major force in the segment, Google has thrown its hat into the ring with the Pixel Fold, which means, going forward, we'll hopefully have something new to look forward to each year.

While the company did spend years developing its first foldable smartphone, it isn't quite the home run we all had hoped for, but there's still a lot to like about the device like its familiar look, ergonomic design, and impressive cameras. With that said, because it's a foldable, you're going to be paying a premium for the device, with the base 256GB model coming in at $1,799, and the 512GB version coming in just a touch higher at $1,919.

Although these are quite expensive, these aren't figures out of the norm, and are on par with some of the best foldable smartphones currently on the market. The phone has been available to purchase since June and can be found at major retailers and wireless carriers. While the best deals could be had during the preorder process, that doesn't mean you still can't save by purchasing the phone right now. So, if you've been looking for the best deals on the Pixel Fold, you've come to the right place.

Google Pixel Fold: Best places to buy the phone unlocked

Amazon

The online retailer offers the Obsidian color of the Pixel Fold in both 256GB and 512GB variants. While the 512GB model doesn't have any discounts right now, the 256GB version does come in slightly below retail, coming in at $1,749. That means, for a limited time, you can save yourself $50 by picking it up from Amazon. Of course, spending that much at once can be a real hit on the wallet, so if you're a credit card holder with Amazon you can always take advantage of special financing offers.

Google Pixel Fold
$1749 $1799 Save $50

The Pixel Fold is available in Obsidian and comes in two models with storage sizes coming in at 256GB and 512GB.

$1749 at Amazon

Google

The Google Pixel Fold is being offered directly from the Google Store, and if you didn't take advantage of the initial promotions and discounts, for now, it looks like you're going to be out of luck. Although there's no special pricing available, there are some financing options for those who qualify, with Google allowing equal payments with zero percent interest over 24 months. Also, if you're a Google One subscriber you can earn 3% credit back with your purchase. You can further lower the price of the phone if you have an older phone to trade in, as you get a nice discount on the device depending on what handset you're willing to part with.

Google Pixel Fold

The Pixel Fold can now be purchased directly from the Google Store with special financing options available. Google One members can earn 3% back in credit with qualifying purchases.

$1799 at Google Store

Google Pixel Fold: Best places to buy the carrier-locked model

AT&T

AT&T is now offering up to $1,000 off the Pixel Fold when signing up for a qualifying wireless plan and trading in an eligible device. The $1,000 credit is issued over 36 months, which means you're going to be locked in with AT&T in order to take advantage of this promotional plan. With that said, if you qualify, you'll be paying $24.45 per month, which isn't all that bad. Of course, just make sure that your area has great service with AT&T before signing up for this promotion.

Google Pixel Fold

The Google Pixel Fold is a feature-rich device with a compact form factor, flagship hardware, impressive cameras, and a phenomenal software experience. 

$1880 at AT&T

Verizon

Verizon is also offering up to $1,000 credit when signing up for an eligible plan and trading in of an older device. The wireless carrier does have the 256GB or 512GB models available in Obsidian.

Google Pixel Fold

Get up to $1,000 off the Google Pixel Fold with a trade-in and signup for an eligible plan.

$1800 at Verizon

T-Mobile

T-Mobile has the 256GB model of the Pixel Fold available in Obsidian and as far as pricing goes, the wireless carrier is currently running a promotion that will knock $800 off the retail price if you sign up for a new Go5G Plus line of service or add a new line of service to your existing plan.

As you might surmise, T-Mobile isn't going to just knock $800 off the price of the phone or hand you cash, instead, it's going to offer 24 monthly bill credits. That means you'll have to stay locked in with T-Mobile over the next two years. For most, this shouldn't be an issue. But if you happen to cancel or move to a new carrier during this promotional period, you will lose out on whatever promotional credits are left.

Google Pixel Fold

The Google Pixel Fold offers a large form-factor foldable with a wider outer display than its competition while also being thinner. The impressive device sports some of the best cameras you'll find in a folding phone. With T-Mobile you could save $800 on it if you act fast.

$1800 at T-Mobile

The Pixel Fold isn't a perfect smartphone by any means, but if you're someone who wants all the perks of a Pixel device in a foldable form factor, there's no better smartphone on the market. Although it won't topple Samsung this year to take the crown when it comes to foldable smartphones, competition like this is good, as it is bringing some much-needed changes to the foldable phone space.

As stated in our Pixel Fold review, the phone is a very good first try from Google, offering plenty of things to love when it comes to the software, hardware, and experience. So if you've been looking to try out a new foldable, the Pixel Fold might be right up your alley.

item.title: Best accessories for Chromebooks in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best accessories for Chromebooks in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There are many great ways you can accessorize a Chromebook, just like a Windows laptop. If you use your Chromebook for work or school, you could use it with a docking station or a Thunderbolt dock with an external monitor. Using an external monitor can provide more screen real estate for multitasking, and docking stations can offer additional ports for connecting your favorite accessories.

Of course, you'll also need an excellent mouse and a great mechanical keyboard to complete your workstation to make typing and scrolling more comfortable. It's also useful to have a nice sleeve to carry your Chromebook in on a daily basis, even if you use a backpack. In this list, we'll take a look at the best accessories to use with your Chromebook. From mice to webcams and more, we've got you covered.

  • LG UltraFine 32UN650W
    Best monitor
    $446 at Amazon

    Premium monitor

  • INIU Power Bank

item.content:encoded:
There are many great ways you can accessorize a Chromebook, just like a Windows laptop. If you use your Chromebook for work or school, you could use it with a docking station or a Thunderbolt dock with an external monitor. Using an external monitor can provide more screen real estate for multitasking, and docking stations can offer additional ports for connecting your favorite accessories.

Of course, you'll also need an excellent mouse and a great mechanical keyboard to complete your workstation to make typing and scrolling more comfortable. It's also useful to have a nice sleeve to carry your Chromebook in on a daily basis, even if you use a backpack. In this list, we'll take a look at the best accessories to use with your Chromebook. From mice to webcams and more, we've got you covered.
